rooms.notes:
Probe method: WebFetch (datacenter IP) — so every 403/Cloudflare finding here is a WORST-CASE result; the user's local residential IP + browser UA posture may pass several of them (SalaryExpert, StepStone, Hays are plain 403s likely UA/IP-filterable; Zippia is a genuine Cloudflare JS challenge and will likely need a real/headless browser). Fetch budget: 11 fetches across 9 sites, all ≤2 per site.

Headline recommendation — wave1 is four connectors that fill our four weakest grid cells: (1) ITJobsWatch for GB (open static percentiles + trends + contractor day rates + per-skill pages; best effort-to-value in the hunt), (2+3) SEEK AU + JobStreet SG as ONE connector — identical Seek-platform template at au.seek.com and sg.jobstreet.com, path /career-advice/role/{slug}/salary, employer-disclosed ad salaries (advertised-kind, complements Adzuna) — covers our two thinnest countries with one parser; note seek.com.au and jobstreet.com.sg both 308-redirect to the new hosts, and (4) Robert Half US+CA (open static Low/Mid/High experience-tier tables; cheapest credible CA source; ~100 curated roles incl. emerging AI titles).

Wave2 pivots on ONE local browser-probe session: Zippia (highest prize — US long-tail + salary-by-experience + explicit career-path ladders with next-role salaries, i.e., our exact goal grid — behind Cloudflare IUAM), SalaryExpert (only single-scheme source spanning all 7 countries with entry/senior split; modeled estimates, label kind=estimate), StepStone/Gehalt.de for DE YoE brackets, ZipRecruiter only if Zippia fails.

New-dimension candidates parked in 'later': freelance/contract rates (Upwork tiered hourly + Malt EU day rates + ITJobsWatch contract pages already give GB day rates in wave1) — this adds an employment-type axis to the grid; and Wellfound equity ranges (startup comp axis). Skips are recorded with reasons so no room re-litigates Comparably/Blind (company-centric charter violation), LinkedIn Career Explorer (dead), Hired (shut down), SalaryExplorer (junk), Naukri (redundant with AmbitionBox).

Ladder-data note: Zippia is the only candidate with true career-path graphs (next role + pay at each step); Seek/JobStreet career-advice role pages have soft progression content worth scraping alongside salaries.

rooms.notes: Probe session summary (5 live probes, all within the ~6-fetch/site budget, browser UA + throttle respected): SEEK AU and ITJobsWatch UK are clean unwalled wave-1 wins with data in exactly our preferred shapes (embedded Apollo JSON; static HTML tables). Canada Job Bank turned out to be a bulk open-data CSV download (2012-2025, NOC x region x low/median/high) — build it as kind=official, not estimate, and it also patches the known gov_projections/official-baseline gap for CA. Gehalt.de is the only probed source with explicit per-YoE-bracket salary for DE (<3/3-6/7-9/>9 Jahre); watch two traps: role slugs are non-obvious (use sitemap/search discovery — wrong slugs soft-redirect to a JS-only A-Z page) and keep a headless fallback in case salary values are partially client-rendered. NodeFlair is Cloudflare-walled to curl AND WebFetch (403/challenge) — demote to wave-2 browser-automation, with MyCareersFuture's public JSON API (mandatory salary on every SG posting) as the reliable SG substitute. Highest-leverage build order for the thin markets: (1) Job Bank CA CSV (hours of work, official), (2) ITJobsWatch (GB roles+skills+trend+skill-adjacency), (3) SEEK connector written domain-generic so jobstreet.com.sg gives SG for free, (4) Gehalt.de (DE YoE brackets). Charter note: kununu only via /gehalt/ role pages, never company pages. Scratch HTML files from probes are in the session scratchpad (nf.html, ijw.html, seek.html, gd.html, gd2.html) if the builder room wants to inspect exact markup.

proposals.name: Catalog data model v2: canonical node + kind + status + unified surface table
proposals.what: Formalize the master catalog on top of what backend/warehouse/taxonomy.py already half-builds. dim_role gains role_kind ('base' | 'specialization') and parent_role_id (specializations point at a base node: 'LLM Engineer' -> parent ml-eng; UI can roll up or drill down), plus status lifted from dim_role_birth ('canonical' | 'emerging' | 'estimate-only' | 'extinct'). Replace/extend dim_role_alias with dim_role_surface: (surface, norm, role_id, source, source_key, lang, weight, first_seen) where source_key is the connector-native id (ambitionbox slug, levels.fyi title id, ESCO conceptUri, O*NET SOC) so every connector joins back to the catalog by key instead of re-matching titles. Seniority and stack/domain stay orthogonal axes exactly as today (dim_seniority, dim_specialization) — that is the mechanism that keeps 3,000 nodes from exploding into 30,000 near-dupes. The 'emerging:' id namespace discipline from mine_emergent_roles is kept; 'estimate-only' is the new status for AmbitionBox long-tail designations that have salary estimates but no posting corpus yet.
proposals.data_sources: Existing curated seed (CURATED_ALIASES, GOV_CROSSWALK), dim_role_birth ledger, connector-native ids from ambitionbox.py SLUGS, levels.fyi payload taxonomy, ESCO occupations_en.csv, O*NET Alternate Titles zip already in staging/onet/.
proposals.data_model: dim_role(role_id, name, role_kind, parent_role_id, family_id, status, is_seed, ord); dim_role_surface(surface_id, surface, norm, role_id, source, source_key, lang, weight, first_seen); dim_role_crosswalk unchanged; dim_role_birth unchanged (append-only). Migration is additive — no existing table breaks.
proposals.product_surface: Invisible spine: resolver, all salary lenses, ladders and coverage marts key on role_id; the catalog browser (separate proposal) reads family -> base -> specialization tree.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — pure role taxonomy; no company axis anywhere in the model. source_key stores designation/occupation ids only, never employer ids.

proposals.name: AmbitionBox designation-universe harvest via sitemap (verified live)
proposals.what: Stop hand-curating the AmbitionBox SLUGS list. Live-probed this session: https://www.ambitionbox.com/sitemap-index.xml -> sitemap-salaries.xml -> sitemap-profilesalaries-1.xml is ONE 2.5 MB XML fetch containing 15,941 designation-salary URLs (/profile/{slug}-salary), including genuine long-tail tech ('ai-engineer', 'machine-learning-scientist-big-data-or-nlp', 'blockchain-developer'). Pipeline: fetch sitemap once per re-crawl cycle -> extract slugs -> run titles through the existing tech_filter.py classifier (built for exactly this: strong-positive / hard-negative word-boundary rules; the universe includes optometrists and counsellors) -> tech subset becomes (a) intake rows for dim_role_surface with source='ambitionbox', source_key=slug, and (b) the connector's crawl frontier, prioritized by whether the slug maps to an existing node (coverage deepening) vs not (catalog growth). Diffing consecutive sitemap snapshots also gives a free new-designation feed for the emerging-role intake.
proposals.data_sources: AmbitionBox sitemap-profilesalaries-1.xml (verified 200, 15,941 <loc> entries, 2026-07-04); backend/ingest/tech_filter.py (reused as-is); existing ambitionbox.py fetch-once cache.
proposals.data_model: staging/ambitionbox/designation_index.parquet (slug, title, is_tech, matched_role_id, match_tier, first_seen_snapshot); feeds dim_role_surface and the connector frontier table.
proposals.product_surface: None directly — it is the enumeration engine that makes 'almost every tech role' reachable for the India-deep market.
proposals.charter_check: PASS with a note — sitemap-companysalaries-*.xml and sitemap-profilecompanysalaries-*.xml sit alongside it and are deliberately NOT fetched (company axis). robots.txt: generic '*' UA allows /profile/ paths (only AI-bot UAs are blanket-disallowed); our approved posture is local residential IP + browser UA, per the standing user ruling — noted honestly, not re-litigated.

proposals.name: Reference-taxonomy harvest: ESCO ICT subset + O*NET alternate titles + levels.fyi families + Zippia
proposals.what: Fill the alias graph and the base-node candidate list from the four reference taxonomies the loaders already anticipate. (1) O*NET: staging/onet/onet_db.zip is already on disk and load_onet_alternate_titles() already parses it — this is a zero-fetch win that adds thousands of US-English surfaces. (2) ESCO: download occupations_en.csv once (plus de/others for multilingual DE aliases); the ICT subset (ISCO groups 25x, 133, 35x) is roughly 150-200 occupations with rich altLabels; load_esco() already exists but currently only maps INTO the 16 curated nodes — extend it to also emit unmapped ICT occupations as base-node CANDIDATES rather than discarding them. (3) levels.fyi: its taxonomy (job families + specialisations, ~60-80 tech families) already ships in payloads the specced connector will fetch — mine it as the authoritative source for level-ladder-bearing roles across all 7 countries. (4) Zippia (optional, wave-2): its browsable career-title index is large but US-only and lower quality; treat as alias enrichment, not node source. Each source lands with its own source tag and weight in dim_role_surface, so provenance-ranked matching keeps curated > onet > esco > lightcast > observed.
proposals.data_sources: staging/onet/onet_db.zip (present), ESCO portal occupations_en.csv (free download, file-drop convention already coded), levels.fyi payload JSON (arrives with the specced connector), Zippia title index (optional crawl, ~6-fetch recon first).
proposals.data_model: All rows land in dim_role_surface + dim_role_crosswalk (esco system already supported); unmapped ESCO ICT occupations land in a role_candidate staging table feeding the admission ladder.
proposals.product_surface: Resolver recall — 'never dead-end' becomes real for German-language queries and obscure US titles; crosswalk confidence tiers surface in the methodology page.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — occupations, titles and labels only; ESCO/O*NET are government taxonomies with no employer dimension.

proposals.name: Admission ladder: surface -> canonical dedup pipeline with base-vs-specialization test
proposals.what: One deterministic pipeline that turns any incoming surface (sitemap slug, ESCO label, derived cluster centroid, user query) into either an alias of an existing node, a new specialization, or a new base-node candidate. Tiers: (1) normalize_surface + exact alias-graph hit -> alias, done; (2) match_title_to_role word-bounded containment -> alias at lower weight; (3) MiniLM embedding vs node centroids (the resolver's tier-3 machinery, reused offline) — cosine >= ~0.85 to an existing node -> alias; 0.70-0.85 -> SPECIALIZATION of that node (role_kind='specialization', parent = nearest node) when its skill fingerprint shares a majority core with the parent but adds a distinct stack/domain cluster, else candidate; (4) below threshold -> base-node candidate awaiting the emerging-role criteria. Every admission writes to dim_role_birth (append-only) with detected_from + provenance JSON, so the catalog is auditable and never silently renumbered. Realistic size at steady state, stated for planning: ~300-600 canonical BASE tech roles (ESCO ICT ~200 is the floor; AmbitionBox tech designations collapse heavily under seniority-stripping), 1,000-2,500 specializations, and 30,000-60,000 surfaces in the alias graph. 'Almost every tech role' is achieved at the SURFACE layer (every title resolves), not by minting tens of thousands of nodes.
proposals.data_sources: All intake feeds from the two enumeration proposals + derived_roles.parquet clusters + observed titles from the LLM-extraction disambiguated_role field.
proposals.data_model: role_candidate(surface, norm, best_node, cosine, skill_overlap, countries, volume, decision, decided_at) staging table; decisions materialize into dim_role / dim_role_surface / dim_role_birth.
proposals.product_surface: None directly; it is what keeps the catalog clean at 10-100x today's size and what the resolver's honesty copy ('Closest match: X') is grounded in.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — clustering is over titles+skills fingerprints; company fields are already excluded from the composite fingerprint.

proposals.name: Emerging-role auto-intake: how 'AI Agent Engineer' gets discovered and admitted
proposals.what: Extend mine_emergent_roles() from one signal to four, with a promotion lifecycle. Signals: (a) derived_roles.parquet clusters (exists today — volume floor + >=2 countries + no alias match); (b) AmbitionBox sitemap diff — a NEW designation slug appearing between snapshots is a strong India-market birth signal with a salary estimate attached from day one; (c) levels.fyi taxonomy diff — new families/specialisations appear there early for US/global comp-ladder roles; (d) skills_emerging co-occurrence — when an emerging skill cluster (e.g. 'agent frameworks', 'MCP', 'LangGraph') starts appearing in postings whose titles do NOT resolve to any node, the unresolved title group is a candidate even below the multi-country floor. Lifecycle: candidate -> status='emerging' (born in dim_role_birth with countries/volume provenance, servable immediately behind an 'Emerging' badge) -> auto-promote to 'canonical' after clearing the volume floor in 2 consecutive quarters -> or age out to 'extinct' (ledger keeps it; the extinction radar is already a designed feature). Concretely for 'AI Agent Engineer': it appears as an unresolved title cluster in CC postings + a new ambitionbox slug; tier-3 cosine lands ~0.78 near ml-eng -> admitted as specialization of ml-eng with status emerging; if its skill core later diverges (agents/orchestration vs training/MLOps) a quarterly re-cluster check flags it for base-node promotion.
proposals.data_sources: derived_roles.parquet, AmbitionBox sitemap snapshots (diff), levels.fyi taxonomy payloads (diff), fact_skill_adoption / skills_emerging, unresolved-query log from the resolver (what users searched that hit tier 3 is itself a demand signal).
proposals.data_model: Reuses dim_role_birth (append-only, EMERGING_PREFIX namespace) + role_candidate; adds a tiny catalog_snapshot table (source, snapshot_date, n_entries, diff_added) for the diff feeds.
proposals.product_surface: An 'Emerging roles' rail (born-this-quarter list with first-seen date, countries, and whatever lens data exists) — a genuinely differentiating, decision-relevant surface for people picking a career direction.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — emergence is measured in postings/countries/skills volume, never 'which companies are hiring it'.

proposals.name: Per-cell coverage mart + honest sparse rendering (kill the silent role-drop)
proposals.what: The UI currently drops sparse roles (src/app/explore.jsx lines 66 and 78: `if (!cd) return null` omits a role from cross-country grids when any country cell is missing — commit ec41ca7 made this the blanket fix for the blank dashboard). Replace with per-cell truth. Backend: materialize mart_role_coverage(role_id, country, lens [advertised|realized|official|estimate], yoe_bucket, n_datapoints, n_sources, last_updated) at build time — it is a cheap GROUP BY over the existing fact tables. API: role payloads always include every catalog role the resolver can land on, with per-country per-lens coverage stamps instead of missing keys. UI: (a) grid/compare cells render a neutral 'no data yet' cell (distinct from zero!) with a tooltip naming which lenses DO have data; (b) role pages show a coverage strip (7 countries x 4 lenses grid of filled/empty dots); (c) estimate-only roles render fully with the kind=estimate badge already mandated by the aggregator-wave rules; (d) sort/rank views rank only over cells with data but never remove the row. This is the prerequisite for growing the catalog at all — at 500+ roles nearly everything is sparse somewhere, and the current drop behavior would blank the product.
proposals.data_sources: Existing fact tables (salary lenses, demand, adoption) + dim_role_surface; no new external data.
proposals.data_model: mart_role_coverage as above, refreshed in the marts build; one added field on role API payloads (coverage: {country: {lens: n}}).
proposals.product_surface: Every grid, compare view and role page; the coverage strip doubles as the product's honesty signature.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — coverage is per role x country x lens x experience; no company dimension.

proposals.name: Catalog browser: family -> base role -> specialization tree with coverage chips
proposals.what: A navigable front door for the full catalog once it grows past what a flat 16-role list can carry. Tree view: family (Engineering / Data / ML-AI / Infra / Security / Product-Design) -> base roles -> specializations, each node carrying its coverage chips (which countries, which lenses, emerging/canonical badge, first-seen year for emerging nodes). Search stays the never-dead-end resolver; the browser is for discovery ('what roles exist NEAR data engineering and what do they pay at my YoE'). Cheap because it reads dim_role (kind/parent/family/status) + mart_role_coverage only. Also the natural home for the ladder view: clicking a node shows what-you-become-next from bridge_role_ladder + promotion_ladder, which closes the loop on the goal grid (role x country x YoE x ladder-step).
proposals.data_sources: dim_role v2, mart_role_coverage, bridge_role_ladder, promotion_ladder — all internal.
proposals.data_model: No new tables; one API endpoint serving the tree with coverage annotations.
proposals.product_surface: New 'All roles' page replacing the implicit 16-role assumption in src/app/roles.jsx; entry point for the emerging-roles rail.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — the tree is roles/families/specializations only.

rooms.notes: Live-probe findings (2026-07-04, within budget: 2 WebFetch datacenter attempts failed as expected — AmbitionBox hangs and levels.fyi guessed-path 403s from datacenter IPs — then 7 small local fetches with browser UA at ~3s spacing): (1) AmbitionBox robots.txt points to /sitemap-index.xml; sitemap-salaries.xml splits into companysalaries (ignored, charter), profilecompanysalaries (ignored, charter), and sitemap-profilesalaries-1.xml — a SINGLE 2.5 MB file with exactly 15,941 designation-salary URLs. The whole India designation universe is enumerable in one polite fetch; no crawling of index pages needed. It spans all professions, so backend/ingest/tech_filter.py (already built for exactly this shape of problem) gates intake; expect roughly 3-6k tech-ish designations collapsing to low-thousands of surfaces after normalize_surface. (2) AmbitionBox robots '*' UA permits /profile/ paths; only named AI-bot UAs are blanket-disallowed — consistent with the standing approved posture. (3) Key code anchors for whoever builds: taxonomy spine D:\A1- Job market\backend\warehouse\taxonomy.py (normalize_surface, CURATED_ALIASES, mine_emergent_roles, EMERGING_PREFIX namespace, load_esco/load_onet loaders already graceful-degrade); resolver 3-tier cascade D:\A1- Job market\backend\app\resolver.py; cluster miner D:\A1- Job market\backend\ml\role_derivation.py (derived_roles.parquet contract); UI sparse-drop to remove: D:\A1- Job market\src\app\explore.jsx lines 66 and 78 (`if (!cd) return null`). (4) Sizing answer the room was asked for: canonical BASE nodes land in the low hundreds (300-600), specializations 1-2.5k, alias surfaces 30-60k — 'almost every tech role' is delivered at the surface/alias layer via the resolver, not by minting a node per title; this matches the axes-not-nodes design already documented in taxonomy.py. (5) Build order recommendation: coverage mart + sparse rendering FIRST (proposal 6) — without it, growing the catalog blanks the UI (the exact regression ec41ca7 papered over); then AmbitionBox sitemap harvest (small, verified), then data model v2, then admission ladder, then emerging intake and browser.

proposals.name: Canonical rung spine: dim_ladder_rung + fact_rung_salary
proposals.what: Replace the pay-multiple-only bridge_role_ladder (role_id, ord, title, mult) with a real rung dimension and a rung-level salary fact. dim_ladder_rung(role_id, rung_code, ord, title, track, yoe_min, yoe_max, fork_from, confidence, source_id) with a fixed canonical rung vocabulary: IC track L1_entry, L2_mid, L3_senior, L4_staff, L5_principal, L6_distinguished; management track M1_lead, M2_manager, M3_senior_mgr, M4_director_plus. fact_rung_salary(role_id, rung_code, country_code, year, median, p25, p75, currency_code, sample_size, kind, source_id, is_seed) — kind reuses the existing lens discipline (advertised | realized | estimate | official), lenses shown side-by-side and never blended, matching the three-salary-lens pattern already in the app. This is the spine every other proposal writes into.
proposals.data_sources: Curated seed (migrate existing bridge_role_ladder rows as source_id=curated with mult retained as fallback when no absolute salary exists); then filled by the empirical/fusion proposals below.
proposals.data_model: Two new DDLs in backend/warehouse/schema.py (BRIDGES + FACTS); PK (role_id, rung_code) and (role_id, rung_code, country_code, year, source_id). track VARCHAR ('ic'|'mgmt'); fork_from VARCHAR nullable = rung_code where the management track branches (typically L3_senior). Mart: mart_role_ladder per (role_id, country) with rungs ordered, steps (abs + pct deltas) precomputed exactly like promotion_ladder.py already computes steps.
proposals.product_surface: Foundation — serves the role-page ladder section and the compare view; API GET /roles/{id}/ladder?country=XX returns rungs + steps + per-lens salaries.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — rungs are role-level abstractions; sources are pooled across employers; no company ladder is ever named or surfaced (canonical rung titles are generic, not company-branded).

proposals.name: Empirical rungs from our own LLM extraction (seniority × management_scope × years_required)
proposals.what: We already extract, per posting: seniority (enum), management_scope (ic|lead|manager|director_plus), years_required min/max, plus advertised salary where present. Group the extracted corpus by (role_id, seniority, management_scope, country) → map to canonical rung_code via a small static dict → emit per-rung yoe_min/yoe_max (p25/p75 of years_required) and per-rung advertised salary medians. This is the cheapest at-scale rung × YoE × pay signal we own outright, and it covers niche/new roles the moment postings exist — no external source needed.
proposals.data_sources: Existing llm_extract output over the postings corpus (currently common_crawl; grows as corpus grows). Zero new fetching.
proposals.data_model: New analytics module backend/analytics/rung_empirics.py (same pattern as promotion_ladder.py: pure derivation on cached data → staging json → materialize). Writes dim_ladder_rung yoe bounds (source_id=extraction) and fact_rung_salary rows kind=advertised. Min-cell threshold n>=30 like promotion_ladder's min_rung_n; abstain-aware (seniority=unknown rows dropped).
proposals.product_surface: Fills the ladder for the long tail of roles; also powers the 'expected years at this rung' label (years_required of postings at rung N+1 ≈ cumulative YoE to reach it).
proposals.charter_check: PASS — aggregates over postings with employers already ignored; extraction fields are role-only by prior audit (extraction-schema-complete).

proposals.name: Multi-source rung crosswalk + fusion rules
proposals.what: One bridge table maps every source's native level scheme onto the canonical rungs so ladders fuse instead of fragmenting: H-1B prevailing-wage I–IV (promotion_ladder.py, US realized) → L1/L2/L3/L4-ish; AmbitionBox designation slugs (software-engineer / senior- / lead- / principal-) → L2/L3/M1-or-L4/L5 with their own per-slug YoE+pay (IN estimate); levels.fyi standardized levels when that connector lands (7-country estimate); LLM seniority enum (advertised); curated titles. Fusion rule: crosswalk maps LEVELS only — salaries stay per-source per-kind rows in fact_rung_salary and are displayed as lenses, never averaged across kinds (exactly the existing three-lens ruling). Where two sources of the SAME kind disagree on a rung's pay, keep both rows keyed by source_id and let the mart pick by sample_size for the headline with the other as tooltip provenance.
proposals.data_sources: bridge_rung_crosswalk is hand-curated (~50 rows total — level schemes are tiny). Consumers: promotion_ladder.py output, ambitionbox.py senior-slug rows, future levels_fyi.py, rung_empirics.
proposals.data_model: bridge_rung_crosswalk(source_id, native_level VARCHAR, role_family VARCHAR DEFAULT '', rung_code, weight DOUBLE, PK(source_id, native_level, role_family)). role_family override handles e.g. AmbitionBox 'lead' meaning M1 for eng roles but L4 for QA. Materialize joins through it into fact_rung_salary.
proposals.product_surface: Invisible plumbing that makes the ladder page show US realized + IN estimate + advertised on the same rung rows with per-lens badges.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — crosswalks level VOCABULARIES, not companies; H-1B employers stay pooled; AmbitionBox rows are designation-level aggregates.

proposals.name: IC vs management fork as a first-class graph (rung DAG + cross-role fork edges)
proposals.what: Represent the ladder as a two-track DAG, not a list. Within-role: dim_ladder_rung.track + fork_from gives the branch point (L3_senior → L4_staff OR M1_lead). Cross-role: when the management branch is really a role change (swe → eng-mgr, data-sci → ds-manager), emit it as a bridge_role_adjacency edge with new edge_type='promotion_fork' carrying the target role's M-rung — reusing the existing adjacency table instead of inventing a parallel one. This makes 'stay IC vs go manager' answerable with pay: the fork card shows staff-IC median vs eng-mgr median in the user's country, computed from fact_rung_salary. Empirical grounding: management_scope extraction already separates track from level, so fork YoE (typical years at which manager postings start asking for this role's background) is derivable.
proposals.data_sources: dim_ladder_rung (proposal 1), bridge_role_adjacency (exists, just add edge_type), management_scope × years_required extraction, eng-mgr/pm rungs from H-1B + AmbitionBox (both cover manager designations).
proposals.data_model: No new tables: one enum value on bridge_role_adjacency.edge_type + two columns on dim_ladder_rung (track, fork_from). Mart emits ladder as {ic_rungs:[], mgmt_rungs:[], fork:{at, options:[{rung|role, median, delta_pct}]}}.
proposals.product_surface: Role-page 'The Fork' card at the senior rung: two branches with pay curves diverging; click-through to the manager role's own page.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — role→role edges only (the existing adjacency bridge is explicitly roles-only); no employer career graphs.

proposals.name: Role-page ladder UI: stepped pay curve with rung bands
proposals.what: The role page gets a Progression section: x-axis = years of experience, y-axis = pay (country-selected, currency-native). Rung bands (L1..L5, M-track dashed) overlay the curve; each band shows title, yoe range, median + step delta (▲ +26k, +23%) in the promotion_ladder.py style. For IN, the AmbitionBox per-year YoE series (1–8) draws a smooth line INSIDE the rung bands — the only market where we can show both the continuous curve and the discrete rungs; other countries show stepped rung medians only, honestly. Lens toggle (advertised/realized/estimate) switches which fact_rung_salary kind renders; missing lenses are greyed with a 'no data' note, never imputed.
proposals.data_sources: mart_role_ladder (proposal 1 mart), AmbitionBox per-year rows (fact_salary_job or estimate rows with experience_code per-year), existing three-lens salary marts for the pooled baseline reference line.
proposals.data_model: Frontend-only beyond the mart: one API endpoint /roles/{id}/ladder?country= returning rungs, steps, fork, and the per-year YoE series when present.
proposals.product_surface: New section on every role page; the compare page gets a mini-ladder sparkline (total_climb_pct badge — already computed by promotion_ladder.py).
proposals.charter_check: PASS — visualizes role-level aggregates; per-country and per-YoE axes are explicitly charter-approved.

proposals.name: 'Where can I go from here' explorer (vertical + lateral, with pay deltas)
proposals.what: Interactive panel: user picks current role + rung (or YoE, mapped to rung via yoe bounds). Output: (a) NEXT RUNG UP — title, expected additional years, pay delta; (b) THE FORK if at branch point; (c) LATERAL MOVES — top-k bridge_role_adjacency neighbors (O*NET/ESCO/Wikidata edges, already loaded) each annotated with the pay delta at the EQUIVALENT rung in the target role (join both roles' fact_rung_salary at same rung_code and country) and a skill-gap chip (bridge_role_skill_importance diff: skills the target role needs that the source role doesn't). This is the decision product the goal grid points at: role × country × YoE × next-step, in one screen.
proposals.data_sources: All existing: bridge_role_adjacency, bridge_role_skill_importance, fact_rung_salary (new), O*NET trajectory bridges. No new ingestion.
proposals.data_model: Query-time composition in a service module; optional cache table mart_next_moves(role_id, rung_code, country_code, moves JSON) if latency demands.
proposals.product_surface: Standalone 'Career Explorer' page + a compact version embedded at the bottom of each role page. Deep-links so a lateral-move card navigates to the target role's ladder.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — transitions are occupation→occupation from public taxonomies; skill gaps are role-attribute diffs; no employer data anywhere in the path.

proposals.name: AmbitionBox senior-designation harvest as ladder rungs (extend the built connector)
proposals.what: The AmbitionBox connector already fetches senior-software-engineer / senior-data-scientist / senior-data-engineer slugs because per-year YoE caps at ~5 on base designations. Extend the slug list systematically: for every base role slug, also fetch lead-, principal-, staff-, and -manager variants (404s are cheap, cached, one request each — the connector already handles clean misses via slug_missing). Each hit is a rung with its OWN YoE range and pay distribution → rows straight into fact_rung_salary (kind=estimate, country=IN) through the crosswalk. Gives India — the deep market — the most complete empirical ladder of any country, including for niche roles where designation pages exist but official data never will.
proposals.data_sources: AmbitionBox designation pages via existing __NEXT_DATA__ parser; ~4 extra slugs × ~40 base roles ≈ 160 additional cached fetches at the existing 2.5s throttle (~7 min once).
proposals.data_model: No schema change: extends SLUGS in backend/ingest/ambitionbox.py; a rung_hint field on the staged record (parsed from slug prefix) that the crosswalk consumes.
proposals.product_surface: Directly populates the IN ladder UI rungs above L3; makes the per-year curve + rung bands complete for India.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — designation-level aggregates only, per the connector's existing roles-only stance; aggregator-estimate wave already user-approved, rows labeled kind=estimate.

proposals.name: Time-to-rung estimator (expected years at each rung, cross-validated)
proposals.what: Derive 'typical years to reach rung N' from three independent signals and publish the band, not a fake-precise number: (1) years_required distribution of postings AT rung N (advertised bar); (2) AmbitionBox per-year pay curve inflection points (IN — where the pay slope jumps is where the market prices a rung change); (3) H-1B level-wise wage overlap (US). Store consensus yoe_min/yoe_max per rung; flag rungs where sources disagree by >2 years with confidence=low. Powers the 'you are probably here' marker when a user enters their YoE, and the '~3 yrs to next rung' label.
proposals.data_sources: rung_empirics output, AmbitionBox per-year rows, promotion_ladder.py buckets (expose the raw per-level wage lists it already collects instead of discarding them).
proposals.data_model: Fills dim_ladder_rung.yoe_min/yoe_max + adds yoe_confidence; small analytics module, staging json → materialize like promotion_ladder.
proposals.product_surface: Rung band widths on the ladder chart ARE these estimates; explorer uses them to place the user.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — statistical derivation over already-pooled role-level data.

rooms.notes:
Probe verdict on assigned sources: progression.fyi was probed (site + repo search) — it is a link DIRECTORY to company-branded frameworks (Notion pages, PDFs at gitlab.com, dropbox.design, etc.), not structured data; ingesting it would both be an HTML-scraping slog and drag named-company ladders into a roles-only product. Verdict: do NOT build a connector; use it offline once as a reference when hand-writing the canonical rung vocabulary (L1..L6 / M1..M4 titles). Zippia career-path graphs were not probed (fetch budget spent) but are lower priority than the zero-fetch assets we already hold; treat as wave-2 if lateral-move coverage from O*NET/ESCO/Wikidata proves thin. levels.fyi remains the planned high-value external source and slots cleanly into the crosswalk (proposal 3) — nothing here blocks on it.

Key architectural rulings embedded in the proposals: (1) canonical rung codes are the fusion spine — every source keeps its native levels and maps through bridge_rung_crosswalk, so adding a source is ~10 crosswalk rows, no schema change; (2) salary lenses NEVER blend across kind (advertised/realized/estimate/official) — per-rung rows stay per-source and render as the same lens toggle the app already trains users on; (3) IC/mgmt fork is data (track + fork_from + promotion_fork adjacency edges), not UI hardcoding, because for many roles 'manager' is a role CHANGE and must route to the manager role's own page; (4) the single highest-leverage/lowest-cost item is proposal 2 (rung empirics from our own extraction) — it is pure derivation on data we already have, covers niche roles automatically, and unblocks the UI even before any aggregator wave lands. Suggested build order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 7 → 5 → 4 → 8 → 6. Grounding files: backend/warehouse/schema.py (bridge_role_ladder at line 250, bridge_role_adjacency at 261), backend/analytics/promotion_ladder.py (H-1B I–IV ladders, steps/total_climb_pct math to reuse), backend/ml/llm_extract.py (seniority/management_scope/years_required enums), backend/ingest/ambitionbox.py (senior-slug pattern + per-year cap note at lines 84–88).

rooms.room: Experience Curve Room — end-to-end design of the years-of-experience axis (canonical scheme, curve fitting, seniority reconciliation, progression metrics, storage + marts)

proposals.name: P1 — Interval-native YoE observation layer (fact_salary_yoe_obs + per-source mapping)
proposals.what: Stop forcing sources into the coarse experience_code enum. New fact stores every salary observation with its NATIVE experience interval: columns yoe_min REAL, yoe_max REAL (nullable = pooled), plus median/p25/p75, currency, sample_size, year, kind (estimate | job-level | person-level), source_id. Canonical per-source mapping, applied at ingest: AmbitionBox ?experience=N -> [N,N] exact per-year (IN, 1-8, plus a pooled row); PayScale brackets -> [0,1), [1,5), [5,10), [10,20), [20,30] (top bracket capped at 30 to bound the interval); SO survey YearsCodePro -> exact integer [N,N] where cell n>=8, else fall back to current bands; talent.com tiers and levels.fyi levels do NOT get invented years — they enter with a seniority/level label and null yoe, to be placed on the axis by P3's concordance; H-1B LCA wage levels I-IV likewise enter as level anchors. dim_experience survives only as a display-bucketing helper (add scheme + midpoint columns); it is no longer the storage grain. Existing fact_salary_job/person keep working untouched — this is an additive layer.
proposals.data_sources: AmbitionBox (built, per-year 1-8 IN), PayScale (specced, 5 brackets x 7 countries), SO survey raw CSVs (YearsCodePro re-aggregated per-year), H-1B LCA levels, talent.com tiers, levels.fyi levels (specced), future wave-2 aggregators — every new connector declares its yoe_min/yoe_max mapping in one place (a SOURCE_YOE_MAP dict in the fact loader).
proposals.data_model: DuckDB: fact_salary_yoe_obs(role_id, country_code, yoe_min DOUBLE NULL, yoe_max DOUBLE NULL, level_label VARCHAR NULL, year, median, p25, p75, currency_code, sample_size, kind, confidence, source_id, retrieved_at, PK(role_id, country_code, source_id, year, coalesce(yoe_min,-1), coalesce(yoe_max,-1), coalesce(level_label,''))). dim_experience gains midpoint + scheme columns for legacy band display. No change to schema.py fact_salary_* tables.
proposals.product_surface: Not directly user-facing; this is the substrate every other proposal reads. Indirectly powers the source chips ('AmbitionBox n=1.2k at 5y', 'PayScale 5-9y bracket') on the curve view.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — role x country x years only; employer never appears (H-1B rows are already employer-pooled at ingest; aggregator estimates carry kind=estimate labeling per user ruling).

proposals.name: P2 — Monotone experience-curve fitter with honest bands (answer ANY year)
proposals.what: Per role x country x lens (estimate / advertised / realized — fitted separately, NEVER blended), fit a smooth pay-vs-YoE curve over P1 observations so the UI can answer 'what at 5 years?' everywhere. Method: (1) expand each interval observation to its covered integer years with weight w = log1p(sample_size)/interval_width; (2) weighted isotonic regression on (year, median) to enforce monotone non-decreasing pay (economically sound for 0-20y; flat late-career plateaus are allowed since isotonic permits equal steps); (3) PCHIP smoothing through the isotonic step means to get a differentiable curve; (4) confidence band = weighted residual quantiles per year, widened by a support penalty. Sparse cells borrow shape hierarchically: role x country -> role family x country -> same role global-shape rescaled by the country's pooled-median ratio, with shrinkage weight n/(n+k). Every fitted year gets a support flag: observed (inside bracket coverage), interpolated (between observed years), borrowed (family/global shape). HARD RULE: never extrapolate beyond max observed yoe + 1; the curve simply ends and the UI stops the line — no wild extrapolation.
proposals.data_sources: fact_salary_yoe_obs (P1) only; pure derived analytics, no new fetching. Runs as a warehouse build step after fuse.
proposals.data_model: DuckDB: fact_salary_curve(role_id, country_code, kind, yoe INTEGER 0..20, fit_median, lo, hi, support VARCHAR, n_effective DOUBLE, method VARCHAR, run_id, PK(role_id, country_code, kind, yoe)). Small: ~500 roles x 7 countries x 3 lenses x <=21 years, bounded ~200k rows worst case, realistically far fewer.
proposals.product_surface: The headline 'Experience curve' chart per role x country: line + shaded band, dashed styling where support != observed, curve ends where data ends. A year slider/scrubber reads fit_median/lo/hi at any integer year — the user's '5 years answerable everywhere' requirement.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — purely role x country x year analytics; three lenses stay side-by-side per existing never-blend doctrine; honesty preserved via support flags and the no-extrapolation rule.

proposals.name: P3 — Seniority <-> YoE concordance bridge (from LLM extraction, per role x country)
proposals.what: Resolve the '5y Data Scientist vs Senior Data Scientist' overlap empirically instead of by fiat. For each role x country x seniority enum value, aggregate the LLM-extracted years_required/[.._max] intervals across postings into a YoE distribution: p25/p50/p75 + n. This is the market's OWN definition of what 'senior' means per role per country (senior at 5y in IN vs 8y in DE becomes visible data, not an assumption). The bridge is then the universal adapter that places level-labeled sources (levels.fyi levels, talent.com tiers, H-1B I-IV rungs, curated bridge_role_ladder steps) onto the years axis: a level maps to the concordance's year-interval, entering P2's fitter as interval observations with widened uncertainty. Fallback chain for sparse cells: role x country -> role x global -> family x global.
proposals.data_sources: Existing LLM extraction output (19 role-only fields incl. seniority, management_scope, years_required min/max) — zero new collection; H-1B LCA levels as a US cross-check anchor.
proposals.data_model: DuckDB: bridge_seniority_yoe(role_id, country_code, seniority VARCHAR, yoe_p25, yoe_p50, yoe_p75, n INTEGER, source_id DEFAULT 'llm_extract', run_id, PK(role_id, country_code, seniority)). Country_code '' = global fallback row.
proposals.product_surface: Seniority band overlay on the P2 curve (shaded vertical regions: 'junior | mid | senior | staff+' with their empirical year ranges); tooltip on the year slider: 'At 5 years in India, 68% of Data Scientist postings asking 5y say Senior'. Also the machinery that lets levels.fyi data appear on the years axis at all.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — seniority is a role attribute extracted from postings; no employer dimension (levels.fyi company-level data would be pooled to country-level before entering, consistent with roles-only).

proposals.name: P4 — Demand-side YoE distribution ('what the market asks for')
proposals.what: A second, distinct use of years_required: not pay, but demand shape. Per role x country x year, histogram the years_required intervals across postings -> 'employers hiring this role ask for: 0-2y: 18%, 3-5y: 47%, 6-9y: 28%, 10+: 7%' plus median-asked-years. This answers a different career question than the pay curve: WHERE the openings are on the experience axis (a role can pay well at 10y but have almost no 10y openings). Cheap to compute, entirely from data already extracted, and it gives the experience axis a demand lens matching the existing salary lenses.
proposals.data_sources: LLM extraction over CommonCrawl postings corpus (already run); recompute per future extraction run. No new collection.
proposals.data_model: DuckDB: fact_demand_yoe(role_id, country_code, year, yoe_bucket VARCHAR (0-2|3-5|6-9|10+), share DOUBLE, postings_n INTEGER, median_asked_years DOUBLE, source_id, PK(role_id, country_code, year, yoe_bucket)). Buckets reuse dim_experience codes here since postings state ranges, not points.
proposals.product_surface: 'Who's hiring at your level' mini-bar-chart beside the pay curve; slider integration: at year N show both expected pay AND share of openings asking ~N years. Cross-role compare: 'roles friendliest to 0-2y candidates'.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — aggregated posting counts per role x country; no employer identity anywhere.

proposals.name: P5 — Progression-rate & plateau metrics (switching-premium signal)
proposals.what: Derive decision-grade scalars from the P2 curve per role x country: (1) marginal_growth_pct per year = d(fit_median)/dyear as %; (2) early_momentum = CAGR years 1->5; (3) plateau_year = first year where marginal growth stays under a threshold (e.g. <2%/yr for 2 consecutive years) — 'when does this role stop rewarding tenure'; (4) ladder_step_premium = pay jump implied by crossing each P3 seniority boundary (curve value at band-median years, next band vs current) — the switching/promotion premium; (5) pay_multiple_10v1 = curve(10)/curve(1). Computed only over support=observed|interpolated years — borrowed segments excluded from metrics so we never advertise a growth rate we inferred from another country's shape. These become sortable product metrics: 'fastest-growing-pay roles in DE', 'roles that plateau early in IN'.
proposals.data_sources: fact_salary_curve (P2) + bridge_seniority_yoe (P3). Pure derivation, no collection.
proposals.data_model: DuckDB: fact_progression(role_id, country_code, kind, early_momentum_pct, plateau_year, pay_multiple_10v1, run_id, PK(role_id, country_code, kind)) plus per-year marginal rates readable straight off the curve grid (no extra table needed). Ladder-step premiums land as extra columns on the mart row (JSON) rather than a new fact.
proposals.product_surface: 'Momentum' badges on role cards; a compare-mode column ('+11%/yr early-career vs +6%/yr'); plateau-year annotation on the curve; ladder view enriched with 'next step ~= +22% at ~year 6'. Feeds job_score as an optional opportunity component later.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — role-level economics only; 'switching premium' means switching LEVEL/ROLE, not employer, and is computed from role curves, not company moves.

proposals.name: P6 — mart_experience_curve + API + year-slider UI
proposals.what: Serving layer: one SQLite mart row per role x country carrying the full curve as JSON arrays (years[], per-lens median[]/lo[]/hi[]/support[]), seniority bands JSON (from P3), demand-YoE histogram JSON (from P4), and the P5 scalars — a single-row read renders the whole experience tab. FastAPI endpoint /roles/{id}/experience?country=IN returns it; a point endpoint ?years=5 evaluates the curve server-side for programmatic use. Frontend: new Experience section on the role page — curve chart with band + lens toggle (estimate/advertised/realized), draggable year scrubber with a big 'at 5 years: ₹18.4L (₹15.1–22.0L, medium confidence)' readout, seniority band overlay, openings-by-experience bars, momentum/plateau badges, and per-point source chips for provenance.
proposals.data_sources: fact_salary_curve, bridge_seniority_yoe, fact_demand_yoe, fact_progression — mart build step in materialize.py, no collection.
proposals.data_model: SQLite: mart_experience_curve(role_id, country_code, payload JSON, updated_run_id, PK(role_id, country_code)); payload documented shape versioned with a schema_version key so the frontend can evolve safely. Pydantic schema in app/schemas.py mirrors it.
proposals.product_surface: The user-visible deliverable of this room: every role x country page answers ANY year of experience with pay + band + confidence + seniority context + demand context, with honest gaps where data ends.
proposals.charter_check: PASS — role x country x years surface; provenance chips satisfy the confidence/provenance doctrine; no employer facet anywhere in payload.

rooms.notes: Dependency order: P1 -> (P3, P4 in parallel; P3 needs only existing LLM output so it can start immediately too) -> P2 (consumes P1 intervals + P3-anchored level sources) -> P5 -> P6. Key design commitments: (1) intervals, not bracket enums, are the storage truth — the coarse dim_experience codes become display-only, which future-proofs the schema for any new aggregator's bracket scheme without migrations; (2) level-labeled sources (levels.fyi, talent tiers, H-1B I-IV) are never assigned invented years at ingest — they are placed on the axis only through the empirical P3 concordance, keeping the years axis honest; (3) the three salary lenses stay separate through the entire pipeline — three curves, one chart, toggleable, per existing never-blend doctrine, with aggregator rows in the estimate lens per the user's kind=estimate ruling; (4) monotone isotonic + PCHIP was chosen over free splines because bracket medians from mixed sources are noisy and non-monotone wiggles would read as false insight — the plateau (equal isotonic steps) is the only permitted flatness; (5) the no-extrapolation rule (curve ends at max observed +1 year; borrowed-shape years flagged and excluded from P5 metrics) is the room's honesty backbone. Grounding files: D:\A1- Job market\backend\warehouse\schema.py (dim_experience lines 43-49, salary facts 90-123), D:\A1- Job market\backend\ml\llm_extract.py (years_required/seniority fields, lines 141-144, 186-189), D:\A1- Job market\backend\ingest\ambitionbox.py (EXPERIENCE_YEARS 1-8), D:\A1- Job market\backend\ingest\so_survey.py (YearsCodePro handling), D:\A1- Job market\backend\analytics\promotion_ladder.py (H-1B level rungs I-IV), D:\A1- Job market\backend\marts\materialize.py (currently reads only experience_code='pooled' — P6 replaces that limitation for this surface). Risk note: AmbitionBox per-year data is IN-only, so IN curves will be visibly richer than other countries; the support-flag + shrinkage design makes that asymmetry legible rather than hidden, and PayScale (7-country, 5 brackets) is the priority connector to close the gap for US/GB/CA/AU/SG/DE.

proposals.name: City dimension (salary + demand by city within country)
proposals.what: Add dim_city and a city-grained salary fact so IN users can compare Bengaluru vs Pune vs NCR for the same role x YoE, and US/DE users see hub premiums. Combine with col_index already in dim_ppp for a 'real purchasing power by city' view — the single most decision-relevant cut we don't have.
proposals.data_sources: AmbitionBox publishes per-city salary stats on the same __NEXT_DATA__ pages the built connector already parses (near-zero marginal fetches for IN); PayScale has /research/{country}/City=... pages; our own Adzuna postings and llm_extract corpus carry location strings ready to geocode-normalize; Numbeo col_index already lands in dim_ppp.
proposals.data_model: New dim_city (city_id, country_code, name, col_index) + fact_salary_job_city (role_id, city_id, experience_code, year, median, sample_size, kind, source_id) mirroring fact_salary_job's provenance columns. Keep city out of the main fact PK — it's a drill-down grain, not the default grain.
proposals.product_surface: A 'by city' tab on the role page: bar chart of median by city with sample-size badges, plus a COL-adjusted toggle. City filter on the country explorer.
proposals.charter_check: Pure geography axis, no employer entity anywhere. City is exactly the kind of dimension the charter blesses ('salary-by-city fine').

proposals.name: AI-exposure score per role
proposals.what: Attach a published AI-automation/augmentation exposure index to every role, with an augment-vs-replace framing. In 2026 'will AI eat this role' is arguably THE question a person choosing a tech career asks; we can answer it with citable research rather than vibes.
proposals.data_sources: Felten-Raj-Seamans AIOE scores (public, keyed to SOC), OpenAI 'GPTs are GPTs' occupation exposure ratings, Anthropic Economic Index occupation usage shares, ILO GenAI exposure gradients — all keyed to O*NET-SOC codes we already bridge roles to for trajectory/importance data. One-time static ingest, no crawling.
proposals.data_model: fact_role_ai_exposure (role_id, exposure_index 0-100, augmentation_share, replacement_share, source_id, year, methodology_note). Multiple sources kept side-by-side like the three salary lenses — never blended into one fake number.
proposals.product_surface: An 'AI exposure' chip on the role card with a click-through explaining which source says what; a sortable exposure column in the role catalog; pairs naturally with fact_role_outlook growth projections.
proposals.charter_check: Role-level attribute from occupation research; no employer axis. Provenance-first presentation keeps it honest — we present named indexes, not our own prediction.

proposals.name: Skill pay premium per role x level (hedonic)
proposals.what: Which skills actually move pay for this role at this level — e.g. 'Data Engineer + Spark in IN at 3-5y: +12% vs role median'. Turns the skill bag from descriptive into prescriptive: the learn-this-next answer.
proposals.data_sources: Two independent lenses: (a) our own corpus — llm_extract skills x salary on the same posting rows enables a hedonic regression we fully control; (b) PayScale's by-skill dimension pages (Skill=X) for the roles/countries where our sample is thin, already in the specced PayScale connector's site family.
proposals.data_model: fact_skill_premium (role_id, skill_id, country_code, experience_code, premium_pct, sample_size, method 'hedonic'|'payscale', confidence, source_id). Suppress rows below a sample floor rather than shipping noise.
proposals.product_surface: On the role page skill list: a green +% badge per skill, sortable; a 'highest-paying skills for this role' strip. Feeds the ladder view ('skills that get you to the next step').
proposals.charter_check: Explicitly on the charter's blessed list ('salary-by-skill fine'). No employer entity in either source path.

proposals.name: Remote vs onsite pay gap per role
proposals.what: For each role x country: median salary split by work_arrangement (remote/hybrid/onsite) and the % of postings offering each. Answers both 'can I get this role remote?' and 'what does remote cost me?'.
proposals.data_sources: Entirely from data already landed: llm_extract work_arrangement + posting salary on the same rows. Zero new collection — this is an analytics pass over the existing corpus.
proposals.data_model: fact_salary_arrangement (role_id, country_code, arrangement, experience_code, year, median, share_of_postings, sample_size, source_id). Same provenance columns as the other salary facts.
proposals.product_surface: A three-way split bar on the role page (remote/hybrid/onsite share + median each); a 'remote-friendly' filter/sort in the catalog.
proposals.charter_check: Work-arrangement is a role/posting attribute; no employer axis. Cheapest high-value item on this list — do it first.

proposals.name: Comp composition by level (base / stock / bonus)
proposals.what: At each ladder step, what fraction of total comp is base vs equity vs bonus — the thing that makes a 'senior' offer in the US incomparable to one in IN, and the biggest blind spot of advertised-salary lenses.
proposals.data_sources: levels.fyi per-level TC breakdowns (connector already SPECCED in this wave — this proposal just says: capture the components, not only the total). Coverage honest-labeled: dense for US big-tech-style ladders, thin for IN services companies.
proposals.data_model: fact_comp_mix (role_id, country_code, level_ord, base_median, stock_median, bonus_median, total_median, sample_size, kind='estimate', source_id). Joins bridge_role_ladder on (role_id, ord) so the ladder view can show mix per rung.
proposals.product_surface: Stacked bar per ladder step in the existing ladder/promotion view; a 'how much of this is stock?' tooltip on TC figures.
proposals.charter_check: levels.fyi data arrives company-tagged — MUST be aggregated to role x level x country before landing; company names never persist past the staging layer (same rule as the existing employer dedup key). Aggregated, it is a clean role attribute.

proposals.name: Demand-supply tension index
proposals.what: Ratio/index of employer demand (postings volume) to aspirant supply proxy (search/learner interest) per role x country x year. Flags 'everyone is learning this but hiring is flat' (crowded) vs 'hiring outpaces interest' (underrated) — a genuinely differentiating signal no aggregator shows.
proposals.data_sources: Zero new collection: fact_demand (CommonCrawl/GHArchive postings) and fact_interest (GTrends) already share the role x country x year grain.
proposals.data_model: Either a derived mart view or fact_tension (role_id, country_code, year, tension_index normalized 0-100, demand_component, interest_component, confidence). Must document the interest-as-supply-proxy caveat in dim_source notes.
proposals.product_surface: A 'crowded vs underrated' quadrant scatter (demand vs interest) on the country dashboard; a tension chip on role cards; could feed opp_score in fact_job_score.
proposals.charter_check: Derived from two role-level facts we own; no new source, no employer axis. Honesty requirement: label it an index with a stated proxy, not 'competition ratio'.

proposals.name: Education & certification ROI
proposals.what: Per role x country: share of postings requiring degree vs not, the pay delta between them, and the top named certifications with their frequency and pay delta. Answers 'do I need a Masters?' and 'is CKA/AWS-SA worth it for this role?'.
proposals.data_sources: Already landed: llm_extract education_requirement (enum) + certifications_required (named-credential array) + salary on the same rows. Optional later enrichment from PayScale cert pages.
proposals.data_model: fact_credential_signal (role_id, country_code, credential_type 'education'|'cert', credential_value, posting_share, median_with, median_without, sample_size, source_id). Cert names normalized through a small alias table.
proposals.product_surface: A 'requirements reality' panel on the role page: degree-required %, top-5 certs with frequency + pay delta badges.
proposals.charter_check: Posting-derived role attributes; no employer axis. Pay deltas are correlational — surface with the same confidence-badge honesty as everything else.

proposals.name: Freelance/contract rate lens
proposals.what: Hourly/day-rate medians per role x country as a fourth population lens (kind='freelance'), answering 'what if I contract instead of full-time?' — a real branch in tech career decisions that no current lens covers.
proposals.data_sources: Malt's public rate barometer (FR/DE/ES day rates by role x experience — structured pages, good for DE); talent.com hourly aggregates (already a SPECCED connector — extend it to hourly pages, cheap); Upwork rate pages are bot-walled/JS-heavy, treat as probe-later not planned. Coverage will be genuinely patchy for IN/SG.
proposals.data_model: fact_salary_freelance (role_id, country_code, experience_code, year, rate_median, rate_unit 'hour'|'day', currency_code, sample_size, kind='freelance', source_id). Fourth lens, same never-blend rule as the existing three.
proposals.product_surface: A 'contract' toggle beside the three salary lenses on the role page, shown only where rows exist.
proposals.charter_check: Role x rate only, no client/company axis. Honest caveat: coverage will be EU-heavy; ship with visible gaps rather than pretend completeness.

proposals.name: Language requirements by role x country
proposals.what: Share of postings requiring the local language vs English-only, per role x country. Small but uniquely decision-relevant for the migration use-case (DE and SG especially): 'can I work as a Data Engineer in Germany without German?'.
proposals.data_sources: Already landed: llm_extract spoken_languages_required over the postings corpus. Zero new collection.
proposals.data_model: Aggregate into fact_language_requirement (role_id, country_code, language, required_share, sample_size, source_id) or fold into a general posting-attributes mart alongside the remote-split rows.
proposals.product_surface: One line on the role x country page: 'DE: 61% of postings require German' with sample size. A filter is overkill; a stated fact is the product.
proposals.charter_check: Posting attribute at role x country grain; no employer axis. Only worth doing because the marginal cost is near zero — the field is already extracted.

proposals.name: Gender pay gap per role (published stats only)
proposals.what: Role-level (or role-family-level) gender pay gap figures from published statistical sources, shown as context on the role page. Socially valuable but honestly a contextual stat, not a role-choice driver in our grid.
proposals.data_sources: Official-only route: ONS ASHE gender-gap-by-occupation tables (GB, excellent), BLS median-earnings-by-sex-by-occupation (US), Eurostat/Destatis (DE, coarser). PayScale's gap report is topline-level, not reliably per-role x country — do NOT plan on it. IN/SG per-occupation data effectively unavailable; ship 2-3 countries honestly rather than fake seven.
proposals.data_model: fact_gender_gap (role_id or family_id, country_code, year, gap_pct, basis 'median_hourly'|'median_annual', sample_size, source_id) — occupation-coded official rows mapped through the existing SOC/ISCO role bridges.
proposals.product_surface: A small context stat with source citation on the role page for covered countries; absent (not zero) elsewhere.
proposals.charter_check: Occupation-level official statistics; no employer axis. Verifiability is the constraint — restrict to official sources or drop it.

rooms.notes:
Killed, with reasons: (1) Interview difficulty/process length — the only real source is Glassdoor interview reviews, which are company-anchored (employer-axis at the data grain, charter-hostile even if aggregated), bot-walled, and self-selected; unverifiable at role x country grain. Kill, don't park. (2) Layoff/job-security exposure — layoffs.fyi and WARN filings attribute to companies, not roles; role attribution would be invented. AI-exposure (#2) plus fact_role_outlook shortage flags already cover the defensible part of 'is this role safe'. (3) Work-hours/work-life-balance per role — OECD/official hours-by-occupation data is too coarse (major occupation groups) to say anything at our role grain.

Sequencing observation: proposals 4, 6, 7, 9 (remote gap, tension index, credential ROI, language share) are all zero-new-collection analytics passes over facts already landed — together they're roughly one 'posting-attributes mart' work item and could ship this wave without touching the connector queue. Proposal 1 (city) piggybacks on the already-built AmbitionBox connector's existing page payloads, so its marginal crawl cost for IN is near zero — the effort is schema + normalization, not collection. Proposal 5 (comp mix) is a spec amendment to the not-yet-built levels.fyi connector — cheapest if decided before that connector is written, which is why it's flagged now.

Grounding: schema at D:\A1- Job market\backend\warehouse\schema.py (dim_ppp already carries col_index per country-year — city COL extends this, doesn't invent a new mechanism; fact_demand and fact_interest already share the grain the tension index needs). Extraction fields confirmed present in D:\A1- Job market\backend\ml\llm_extract.py: work_arrangement, education_requirement, certifications_required, spoken_languages_required, management_scope, seniority.

synthesis:
# STRATA MASTER PLAN — Grid Completion: role × country × YoE × ladder

Synthesized from all six rooms. Grounded against `backend/warehouse/schema.py`, `backend/warehouse/taxonomy.py`, `backend/marts/models.py` (MartRolePayLadder, mart_role_ladder), `backend/analytics/promotion_ladder.py`, `backend/marts/materialize.py`.

## 1. NEW SOURCES — the definitive connector build order

## 2. ROLE CATALOG — chosen design

**Target size (planning commitment):** 300–600 canonical base roles, 1,000–2,500 specializations, 30,000–60,000 alias surfaces. "Almost every tech role" is delivered at the **surface layer** — every title resolves via the resolver — not by minting a node per title. Axes (dim_seniority, dim_specialization) stay orthogonal; that is what keeps the catalog from exploding into near-dupes.

**Data model v2 (additive, no breaking migration):** `dim_role` gains `role_kind` (base|specialization), `parent_role_id`, `status` (canonical|emerging|estimate-only|extinct). `dim_role_alias` is superseded by `dim_role_surface(surface, norm, role_id, source, source_key, lang, weight, first_seen)` — `source_key` holds connector-native ids (AmbitionBox slug, ESCO conceptUri, O*NET SOC, levels.fyi family id) so every connector joins by key, never re-matches titles. `dim_role_birth` stays the append-only audit ledger.

**Enumeration feeds (in order of cost):** (1) O*NET alternate titles — the zip is already on disk, `load_onet_alternate_titles()` already parses it: zero-fetch. (2) AmbitionBox sitemap — verified single fetch, 15,941 designation slugs, gated through the existing `tech_filter.py`. (3) ESCO ICT subset (~150–200 occupations + multilingual altLabels; extend `load_esco()` to emit unmapped ICT occupations as base-node candidates instead of discarding). (4) levels.fyi taxonomy when that connector lands. (5) Zippia only as wave-2 alias enrichment.

**Admission ladder (one deterministic pipeline):** normalize + exact alias hit → alias; word-bounded containment → alias (lower weight); MiniLM cosine vs node centroids: ≥0.85 → alias, 0.70–0.85 with shared skill core → specialization of nearest node, else → base-node candidate under the emerging criteria. Every admission writes `dim_role_birth`.

**Emerging intake (4 signals + lifecycle):** derived_roles clusters (exists), AmbitionBox sitemap diffs, levels.fyi taxonomy diffs, emerging-skill clusters attached to unresolved titles. Lifecycle: candidate → emerging (servable behind badge) → canonical after 2 quarters above volume floor → or extinct (ledger keeps it). This is how "AI Agent Engineer" gets in without a human.

**Sparse-coverage rendering (prerequisite for all of the above):** materialize `mart_role_coverage(role_id, country, lens, yoe_bucket, n_datapoints, n_sources, last_updated)`; API payloads always include every resolvable role with per-cell coverage stamps; **remove the silent role-drop at `src/app/explore.jsx` lines 66 and 78 (`if (!cd) return null`)** and render neutral "no data yet" cells (distinct from zero), coverage strips on role pages, estimate-only roles with kind=estimate badges. Without this, growing the catalog blanks the UI — the exact regression ec41ca7 papered over.

## 3. LADDER + EXPERIENCE — one unified data model

The two rooms' proposals are merged into a single five-layer stack. Existing tables are untouched or migrated additively: `fact_salary_job` / `fact_salary_person` / `fact_salary_official` remain the three lens facts; `bridge_role_ladder` (role_id, ord, title, mult) migrates into the new rung spine as curated seed rows (mult kept as fallback); `promotion_ladder.py`'s H-1B I–IV output and `MartRolePayLadder` keep working and become one source feeding the crosswalk; `materialize.py`'s `experience_code='pooled'` limitation is superseded for this surface.

**Layer 1 — Observations (ingest truth).** `fact_salary_yoe_obs(role_id, country_code, yoe_min, yoe_max, level_label, year, median, p25, p75, currency_code, sample_size, kind, confidence, source_id, retrieved_at)`. Interval-native: AmbitionBox `?experience=N` → [N,N]; PayScale brackets → [0,1)…[20,30]; SO YearsCodePro → per-year where n≥8; Robert Half tiers → declared intervals. **Level-labeled sources (levels.fyi, talent tiers, H-1B I–IV) enter with `level_label` and NULL years — never invented years.** Each connector declares one SOURCE_YOE_MAP dict. `dim_experience` becomes display-only.

**Layer 2 — Rung spine.** `dim_ladder_rung(role_id, rung_code, ord, title, track ic|mgmt, yoe_min, yoe_max, yoe_confidence, fork_from, confidence, source_id)` with canonical vocabulary L1_entry…L6_distinguished / M1_lead…M4_director_plus. `bridge_rung_crosswalk(source_id, native_level, role_family, rung_code, weight)` (~50 hand-curated rows) maps every source's native levels onto canonical rungs — crosswalk maps LEVELS only, salaries stay per-source. `fact_rung_salary(role_id, rung_code, country_code, year, median, p25, p75, currency, sample_size, kind, source_id)` is materialized from Layer 1 through the crosswalk. IC/mgmt fork is data: `track` + `fork_from` within-role; cross-role forks (swe → eng-mgr) are `bridge_role_adjacency` edges with new `edge_type='promotion_fork'`.

**Layer 3 — Concordance (the adapter between the two axes).** `bridge_seniority_yoe(role_id, country_code, seniority, yoe_p25, yoe_p50, yoe_p75, n)` aggregated from the LLM-extracted `seniority × years_required` fields we already have — the market's own per-country definition of "senior." This is the ONLY mechanism that places level-labeled observations onto the years axis (as widened intervals feeding Layer 4), and it fills `dim_ladder_rung` yoe bounds cross-validated against H-1B level overlaps and AmbitionBox curve inflections; disagreement >2 years → confidence=low.

**Layer 4 — Fitted curves + derived metrics.** `fact_salary_curve(role_id, country_code, kind, yoe 0..20, fit_median, lo, hi, support, n_effective, method)`: weighted isotonic regression (monotone, plateau-tolerant) + PCHIP smoothing, per lens, **never blended across kinds**; hierarchical shape-borrowing (role×country → family×country → global rescaled) with shrinkage; support flags observed|interpolated|borrowed; **hard rule: no extrapolation beyond max observed YoE + 1 — the curve ends**. `fact_demand_yoe` (share of postings asking each YoE bucket — the demand lens on the experience axis). `fact_progression` (early_momentum CAGR 1→5y, plateau_year, pay_multiple_10v1, per-rung step premiums) computed only over observed|interpolated years.

**Layer 5 — Marts + API + UI.** `mart_experience_curve(role_id, country_code, payload JSON)` — one row renders the whole experience tab: per-lens curve arrays + bands, seniority-band overlay, demand histogram, momentum/plateau badges, per-point source chips. `mart_role_ladder` v2: rungs ordered with per-lens salaries, steps (abs + pct deltas, promotion_ladder.py math reused), fork card `{at, options[]}`. Endpoints: `/roles/{id}/experience?country=&years=` and `/roles/{id}/ladder?country=`. Lateral transitions ("Career Explorer"): query-time composition of `bridge_role_adjacency` (O*NET/ESCO/Wikidata edges, already loaded) + same-rung `fact_rung_salary` deltas + `bridge_role_skill_importance` skill-gap chips; optional `mart_next_moves` cache.

For IN, the AmbitionBox per-year series draws a continuous line inside the rung bands; other countries show stepped rung medians honestly until PayScale/wave-2 fills them.

## 4. EXTRA DIMENSIONS — what makes the cut (core/high only)

1. **Remote vs onsite pay gap** — zero new collection (llm_extract `work_arrangement` × salary); cheapest high-value item in the whole plan, do first.
2. **Demand–supply tension index** — fact_demand × fact_interest already share the grain; "crowded vs underrated" quadrant, labeled as an index with stated proxy.
3. **Education & certification ROI** — zero new collection (`education_requirement`, `certifications_required` × salary); "do I need a Masters / is CKA worth it" panel.
4. **Skill pay premium (hedonic)** — skills × salary on our own posting rows + PayScale by-skill pages; green +% badges per skill, feeds "learn this next."
5. **AI-exposure score per role** — one-time static ingest (Felten-Raj-Seamans, OpenAI, Anthropic Economic Index, ILO) keyed to SOC codes we already bridge; sources side-by-side, never blended.
6. **City dimension** — dim_city + fact_salary_job_city; AmbitionBox pages already carry per-city stats (near-zero marginal crawl for IN); COL-adjusted toggle via existing col_index.
7. **Comp composition (base/stock/bonus)** — decided NOW as a levels.fyi spec amendment (capture components, not just TC); stacked bars per ladder rung.

Freebie: **language requirements** ships inside the same posting-attributes mart pass as items 1 and 3 (field already extracted, marginal cost ~zero). **Cut/parked:** freelance-rate lens (EU-heavy coverage — parked to "later" with Malt/Upwork), gender pay gap (official-source-only, 2–3 countries — parked), interview difficulty / layoff exposure / work-hours (killed, charter or data-quality grounds).

## 5. BUILD ORDER — single sequenced plan, each step ships independently

**Step 0 — Honest sparse rendering (2–3 days). Ships: no more silent role drops.**
`mart_role_coverage` + per-cell "no data yet" rendering; delete the `if (!cd) return null` drops in explore.jsx. Prerequisite for everything that grows the catalog or adds sparse sources.

**Step 1 — Zero-collection analytics wave (~1 week). Ships: 5 new product surfaces from data already on disk.**
(a) Rung empirics from LLM extraction (seniority × management_scope × years_required → rung YoE bounds + advertised rung salaries); (b) `bridge_seniority_yoe` concordance; (c) `fact_demand_yoe`; (d) posting-attributes mart: remote gap + credential ROI + language share; (e) tension index; (f) AI-exposure static ingest. All pure derivation.

**Step 2 — Cheap connector wave for the four weakest countries (~1–1.5 weeks). Ships: CA/GB/AU/SG/DE grid cells.**
Job Bank CA CSVs → ITJobsWatch GB → SEEK AU/JobStreet SG (domain-generic) → Gehalt.de DE. Staging-first; Job Bank fuses immediately into fact_salary_official (also fixes the gov_projections gap).

**Step 3 — Schema v2 landing (~1 week). Ships: the unified ladder+experience substrate, IN ladder complete.**
`fact_salary_yoe_obs` + `dim_ladder_rung` + `bridge_rung_crosswalk` + `fact_rung_salary`; catalog data model v2 (`dim_role` kind/parent/status + `dim_role_surface`); migrate bridge_role_ladder seed and promotion_ladder H-1B rungs through the crosswalk; AmbitionBox sitemap harvest + senior-slug extension (~160 cached fetches); O*NET alternate-titles load (zero fetch).

**Step 4 — Specced aggregator wave (~2 weeks). Ships: cross-country YoE brackets + level ladders + comp mix.**
PayScale (7-country YoE backbone) → Robert Half US/CA → levels.fyi (with comp-mix amendment) → talent.com → MyCareersFuture if SG still thin. All write fact_salary_yoe_obs directly, kind=estimate.

**Step 5 — Curve fitting + progression metrics (~1 week). Ships: "what at 5 years?" answerable everywhere data exists.**
Isotonic+PCHIP fitter → `fact_salary_curve`; `fact_progression`; `mart_experience_curve` + `/experience` endpoint.

**Step 6 — UI wave (~1.5 weeks). Ships: the goal-grid product surfaces.**
Experience tab (curve + band + lens toggle + year scrubber + seniority overlay + demand bars); role-page Progression section (rung bands, IN per-year line, Fork card); catalog browser (family→base→specialization tree with coverage chips) + Emerging-roles rail; Career Explorer (next rung / fork / lateral moves with pay deltas + skill gaps).

**Step 7 — Probe-gated wave 2 (1 probe session + builds as unlocked).**
One local browser session probes Zippia, SalaryExpert, StepStone; build the winners (Zippia's career-path graphs enrich lateral edges; SalaryExpert fills any remaining all-7 holes as kind=estimate); kununu /gehalt/ DE cross-check; admission-ladder pipeline + emerging auto-intake (needs the enlarged surface flow from steps 3–4); NodeFlair only if SG still thin.

**Step 8 — Dimension mop-up.**
Skill pay premium (hedonic), city dimension (schema + AmbitionBox city payloads), then parked items (freelance lens, equity axis) only after the core grid is dense.

Total to a fully-shipped goal grid: roughly 9–10 weeks of sequenced work, with user-visible value landing at every step from Step 0 onward.
